chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: The exact concentration is not provided, though the sources warn that even aside from the flesh, the peels and seeds of fruits often harbor these compounds and must be avoided
chemicals.description: in cats: The sources do not detail the exact cellular mechanism for the specific alkaloids in pineapples, but broadly note that a feline's body simply lacks the ability to safely metabolize these toxic components, leading to a dangerous systemic burden12. (Note: In other plants, the sources note that certain alkaloids act as neurotoxins or severe gastrointestinal irritants34, but the texts do not
chemicals.name: Alkaloids (General Class)

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: The sources do not provide the specific cyanogenic concentration or potency for unripened pineapples
chemicals.description: in cats: Cats cannot safely metabolize cyanides12. When cyanide enters the system, it acts as a potent inhibitor of cytochrome c oxidase, a critical enzyme containing ferric ions located in the mitochondrial electron transport chain56. By binding to this enzyme, cyanide completely halts cellular oxygen metabolism and stops ATP (energy) production, leading to rapid cellular death, which catastrophi
chemicals.name: Cyanides (General Class)

description: Ripe pineapple flesh is not significantly toxic to cats in small amounts, but unripe pineapple and the pineapple plant itself (skin, core, leaves, and stem) are hazardous. Unripe pineapple has very high concentrations of bromelain and oxidative compounds that cause mouth irritation, GI upset, and potentially diarrhea.
emergencyNote: Based on the provided sources, there is no specific, isolated treatment protocol detailed exclusively for unripened pineapples. However
name: Unripened pineapples
safetyNotes: Bromelain risk: unripe pineapple has high bromelain concentrations — a proteolytic enzyme that causes intense oral irritation and mucosal inflammation
Leaves, skin, and core: contain high bromelain plus insoluble fibres that irritate the GI tract; not appropriate for cats
Ripe flesh: very small amounts are not toxic but are high in sugar; cats lack functional sweet taste receptors so show little interest
Symptoms from unripe parts: oral pain, drooling, vomiting, diarrhea
Do not offer unripe pineapple or plant parts; contact your vet if large amounts were consumed

symptoms.name: Difficulty Breathing and Hyperventilation
symptoms.notes: cyanide halts cellular oxygen metabolism rapidly). Any additional clinical notes: The cyanides present in these fruits disrupt normal oxygen utilization, leading to profound respiratory distress and hyperventilation as the body struggles to breathe
symptoms.onset: Rapid onset (exact timeframe is not specified in the sources,

symptoms.name: Vomiting
symptoms.notes: Vomiting is triggered as a severe poisoning symptom and systemic reaction when the cat is unable to metabolize the toxic alkaloids and cyanides ingested from the fruit

symptoms.name: Coma
symptoms.notes: If the cat ingests a highly toxic dose of cyanides and alkaloids from these fruits, it can catastrophically impact the central nervous system, leading to loss of consciousness and a potentially fatal coma
symptoms.onset: Follows initial respiratory distress and systemic poisoning.

treatments.name: Based on the provided sources, there is no specific, isolated treatment protocol detailed exclusively for unripened pineapples. However

treatments.description: of the procedure: Providing the cat with supplemental oxygen to support respiration and cellular function. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Supplemental Oxygen Therapy
treatments.notes: This is a critical supportive measure required for cats suffering from cyanide poisoning, as the cyanogenic toxins found in these fruits compromise the body's oxygen-carrying capacity and completely halt cellular ATP (energy) production

treatments.description: of the procedure: A veterinarian administers a feline-specific medication (such as an α 2 ​ -adrenergic agonist like dexmedetomidine) to safely induce vomiting and expel the unripened pineapple material from the stomach2. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Induction of Emesis (Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This is the primary goal if the ingestion is caught very early (within 1 to 2 hours) and the cat is still asymptomatic. Pet owners must never attempt to use hydrogen peroxide to induce vomiting at home, as it is strictly contraindicated in cats and can cause fatal hemorrhagic gastritis

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering oral activated charcoal to bind to and adsorb any residual toxins remaining in the gastrointestinal tract2. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Administration of Activated Charcoal (Secondary Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This step typically follows successful emesis to trap any remaining alkaloids and cyanides in the gut, preventing them from being fully absorbed into the cat's bloodstream

treatments.description: of the procedure: Hospitalizing the cat to administer continuous IV fluids to maintain organ perfusion, hydration, and electrolyte balance3. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Supportive Care
treatments.notes: Because there is no specific antidote for most feline dietary toxins (including these plant alkaloids and cyanides), treatment relies heavily on intensive supportive care and IV fluids to sustain the cat's vital organs through the systemic poisoning
